Angela Erdmann never met her grandfather because he died in 1946, six years before she was born. However, on Tuesday, April 8, 2014, she shared an amazing story about receiving a message in a bottle that her grandfather had thrown into the Baltic Sea 101 years earlier. This bottle is believed to be the oldest message in a bottle in the world and is now displayed in a museum in Germany.

Angela, who is now 62 years old, was surprised when a man came to her door and said he had mail from her grandfather. He explained that a message in a bottle had been found, and it had her grandfather's name on it. The man was a genealogical researcher who had located Angela in Berlin after the bottle’s message was given to the International Maritime Museum in Hamburg, a city in northern Germany.

The brown beer bottle, which had been in the sea for over a century, was found by a fisherman named Konrad Fischer while he was fishing in the Baltic Sea near the city of Kiel. Holger von Neuhoff, a curator at the museum, said that this bottle and its message are the oldest he has seen. He mentioned that there are older documents in the museum that do not have bottles, but this one is unique because it still has the bottle and is in very good condition.

Police and intelligence agencies have been using polygraphs, or lie detectors, for almost 100 years to help catch criminals and spies. In movies, these machines often show dramatic scenes where guilty people sweat as they answer questions. However, new research from Britain and the Netherlands might make polygraphs outdated.

Scientists have developed a new method that has a success rate of over 70% in tests. This new approach looks at full-body movements instead of just facial expressions or gestures, which are traditionally seen as signs of lying. The polygraph is commonly used in the US for criminal cases and security checks for agencies like the FBI and CIA, but it’s not as popular in Europe. Many experts have questioned the reliability of polygraphs.

The new method, however, has shown better results in experiments. Researchers believe that people who are lying tend to fidget more. They used a special motion suit, similar to what is used in animated movies, to track these movements. One researcher, Ross Anderson, explained that past studies showed interviewers could only tell truth from lies slightly better than random guessing. In contrast, their new method has achieved a success rate of over 70%, and even higher than 80% in some tests. 

Anderson stated, “The main point is that guilty people fidget more, and we can measure this effectively.” The polygraph was invented in 1921 by a policeman named John Larson, based on ideas from psychologist William Marston. The polygraph measures changes in pulse, blood pressure, sweating, and breathing to determine if someone is lying. While films often make the device seem very accurate, the US Supreme Court ruled in 1998 that there is no agreement on its reliability, a view backed by the US National Academy of Sciences in 2003.

Do you want your child to excel in sports, join the school team, and maybe even compete at an international level someday? A study shows that children born in November or October tend to be fitter than those born in other months. Researchers found that kids born in these months are generally stronger, more powerful, and have better stamina compared to their classmates, especially those born in April or June. 

Dr. Gavin Sandercock from Essex University led this study, which included 8,550 boys and girls aged 10 to 16 from various schools in Essex. They were tested on fitness levels, measuring stamina, handgrip strength, and lower-body power between 2007 and 2010. The findings suggest that the month in which a child is born can greatly affect their physical abilities, which are important for sports. 

November-born children were the fittest overall, showing the most stamina and power, and being the second strongest. October-born children were also fit, ranking highest in strength and third in power, followed closely by those born in December. The differences in physical abilities between children born in different months can be significant. For instance, a boy born in November can run at least 10% faster, jump 12% higher, and be 15% more powerful than a boy of the same age born in April. This physical advantage can make a big difference in who becomes a top athlete, as small differences in performance can lead to selection for elite sports.

The study, published in the International Journal of Sports Medicine, indicated that April-born children were the least fit, followed by those born in June. This may lead to these children missing out on school sports teams and not reaching their full potential in sports, according to Sandercock.

The small room looks like a classroom. Its metal walls are decorated with educational posters, including the letters of the alphabet and a map of Bangladesh. However, it's hard to focus because of the loud sound of hammering and the strong chemicals in the air that make your throat itch and eyes water. The children in this tiny room are lucky because they have escaped working in the nearby factories.

For 14 years, SOHAY, a grassroots NGO funded by the Global Fund for Children and Comic Relief, has been helping children in the slums of Dhaka go to school. They focus on kids working in dangerous places like aluminum and plastic factories and tanneries. This classroom is one of 23 centers SOHAY has created in the capital. These centers help children catch up on their education so they can enter primary school. Once they are in school, they can do their homework at the centers with help from their classmates.

Ten-year-old Alamin, who used to work in a plastic factory, goes to one of these centers. His father sells goods on the street, and his mother works part-time as a domestic helper. They are all happy that he is now in school and no longer working in hazardous conditions. His friend Rabi shares that he wants to forget his time in the factory. “I like school,” he says.

SOHAY also helps parents and employers understand why child labor is harmful. They provide skills training to help families earn more money. Mohammed al-Mamun, the program manager of SOHAY, explains, “It is really challenging to get working children into formal education. Their mental and physical health is often different from other children. After work, they sometimes find it hard to make friends and adjust to school.”

Keeping these children in school is difficult too, and many drop out. Seven-year-old Zhorna Akter Sumayya has two older brothers who work (one in a restaurant and one at a local club). However, after learning about education at one of SOHAY’s centers, she now attends a state primary school. Her parents rely on their sons' income but were eager for their daughter to have a chance at an education.

A big disagreement has come up internationally about the right of Bolivia’s indigenous tribes to chew coca leaves, which are used to make cocaine. Bolivia received a special permission from the 1961 agreement that sets the rules for global drug policies, allowing its indigenous people to chew coca leaves. Bolivia argues that this agreement goes against its new constitution from 2009. This constitution states that the country must “protect native and ancestral coca as cultural heritage” and claims that coca in its natural form is “not a dangerous narcotic.” Indigenous people in South America have chewed coca leaves for many centuries. The leaves are believed to provide energy and have medicinal benefits.

People who support Bolivia's position say it is important for defending the rights of indigenous people. Danny Kushlick, from the Transform Drug Policy Foundation, praised Bolivia's decision as a groundbreaking move. He believes it shows that countries can change their approach to international drug laws after tiring of the "war on drugs."

However, the UN’s International Narcotics Control Board (INCB), which monitors drug treaties, says Bolivia's action could harm the international drug control system. Several countries, including the UK, US, Italy, Sweden, the Netherlands, and Russia, are against Bolivia's request. The UK’s statement to the UN acknowledges the cultural significance of coca leaves in Bolivia but also expresses concern. They fear that allowing this exception could lead to increased coca production and, most importantly, more coca being used for cocaine production. They believe this could weaken international laws aimed at fighting illegal drugs and reduce the global response to this issue.

On Black Fell, where you can see Northumberland’s lovely Kielder Water reservoir, a group of people is waiting in a parking lot next to a unique wooden building that looks modern. This is Kielder Observatory, the center of Britain's growing astrotourism. The people waiting outside are the lucky ones, as many others wanted to join the stargazing event but there are only a limited number of spots available.

Inside, by a woodburner and under soft lights, Gary Fildes, the observatory’s founder and lead astronomer, talks to his team. Fildes, who used to work as a bricklayer, shares his thoughts on possibly seeing the northern lights, but he is not very hopeful. Instead, the team decides to focus their powerful telescopes on Jupiter and Venus, and later to spot stars like Capella and Betelgeuse. They are also looking forward to seeing the International Space Station. “Remember,” Fildes tells his team, “it’s about interaction, entertainment, and inspiring people.”

Fildes, who is 49 years old, is leading the way in the UK’s new astrotourism industry. A key moment for Northumberland happened in 2013 when the entire national park that includes Hadrian’s Wall and Kielder Water and Forest Park (which is about 1,500 square kilometers) was given the Dark Sky Park status, making it the only one in England. Dark Sky Parks are special places. In 2013, a Star Count showed that only 5% of people in the UK can see more than 31 stars on a clear night. The International Dark-Sky Association (IDA), based in Tucson, Arizona, gives this status only to places that work hard to reduce light pollution. They also check that the night skies are dark enough.

In Northumberland’s Dark Sky Park, it is so dark that Venus can cast a shadow on the Earth. Duncan Wise, who helps visitors at the Northumberland National Park Authority, played a major role in getting this dark-sky status. “We tend to think about the landscape as everything to the horizon,” Wise said. “But what about what’s above it?” Wise and others spent years preparing their proposal for the IDA, gathering lots of light measurements and teaming up with local councils, parks, and community groups to create a lighting plan that guides how new buildings are designed in the area. Their hard work has paid off. Now, many of the 1.5 million people who visit Northumberland each year know about its Dark Sky status. “We get a lot of people coming here to see the sky now,” says someone at a car rental shop in Newcastle. “They visit in autumn and winter when it’s darkest. This is good for local hotels because they have business all year round now.”

Around 20 years ago, Larry Pizzi first heard about electric bikes and wondered, “Why would anyone want to put a motor and batteries on a bike?” This question is still asked by many traditional cyclists. Bicycle shops have been slow to sell e-bikes, even though they have existed since the late 1990s. In the US, sales have been modest. However, Pizzi, who is now the CEO of Currie Technologies (the top seller of e-bikes in the US), believes this will change soon. Others in the industry agree. Well-known brands like Trek, Raleigh, and Specialized also sell electric models and think the market for e-bikes is about to grow. “We’re close to mainstream adoption,” Pizzi said. “More companies are joining this market every month.”

Compared to other countries, the US is lagging behind in e-bike sales. In 2014, nearly 32 million e-bikes were sold globally, mostly in China, where they are mainly used for transportation. E-bikes are also popular in many parts of Europe. In the Netherlands and Switzerland, for instance, they are common. German postal workers use them, and BMW sells an electric bike for about $3,000.

Electric bikes are not the same as motorcycles or mopeds, which rely entirely on a motor. Instead, e-bikes are bicycles that can be pedaled with some assistance from an electric motor. Riding an e-bike feels like riding a regular bike with a nice tailwind. The motor helps you go faster or climb hills, but you still do most of the pedaling. Unlike mopeds, e-bikes are usually allowed on bike paths and can’t go faster than 20 mph.

Modern scientists, especially chemists, sometimes have to wait a long time for new elements to be discovered. Recently, researchers from Japan, Russia, and the United States found four new elements. This is exciting because it is the first time since 2011 that new elements have been added to the periodic table. The four new elements are very unstable and radioactive, and their discovery updates science textbooks worldwide.

The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), which oversees chemical names and measurements, confirmed these discoveries on December 30, 2015. They reviewed studies that started in 2004. The scientists who discovered these elements need to come up with official names to replace the temporary names based on Latin – ununtrium, ununpentium, ununseptium, and ununoctium – which represent their atomic numbers: 113, 115, 117, and 118. The atomic number shows how many protons are in an element's nucleus.

IUPAC recognized that a team of Russian and American scientists proved the existence of elements 115, 117, and 118. Meanwhile, the discovery of element 113 was credited to scientists from the RIKEN Institute in Japan, making Japan the first Asian country to name an element. According to IUPAC rules, new elements can be named after myths, minerals, places, or scientists.

Unveiling a car that can go only 25 miles per hour, has two seats, and doesn’t have a steering wheel or pedals might not seem very exciting at a car show. However, when Google revealed its latest driverless car in the US, it created a buzz in the car and taxi industries. 

This electric car, which Google is testing near its headquarters in Mountain View, California, does not have the usual controls like foot pedals. Instead, you can use a smartphone app to call the car and set your destination. There is a single STOP button between the two front seats in case the passengers need to take control. The car can handle all driving tasks, including navigation, steering, acceleration, and braking.

Google is making about 100 prototypes for testing over the next two years. Sergey Brin, one of the company’s co-founders, mentioned at a conference that the car is still being developed but aims to help people who currently do not have good transportation options. He described riding in the car as very relaxing, saying, “You’re just sitting there; there’s no steering wheel, no pedals. It reminded me of riding alone on a chairlift. I found it really enjoyable.”

The main goal of this project is to improve safety. Google says the car is built with special materials, like impact-absorbing foam in the front and a plastic windscreen, making it safer for pedestrians than normal cars. These vehicles, specially made by a company in Detroit (whose name has not been revealed), will help further study how driverless cars can best work. Google will continue testing these cars, which are not yet for sale.

Galina Zaglumyonova was sleeping in her apartment in central Chelyabinsk when a huge explosion suddenly woke her up. The blast broke her balcony windows and shattered her clay pots with houseplants. When she got out of bed, she saw a large trail of vapor in the sky and heard car alarms going off outside. “I didn’t understand what was happening,” said Zaglumyonova. “There was one big explosion and then many smaller ones. I first thought it was a plane crash.”

What she actually saw was a ten-ton meteorite crashing to Earth in a series of fireballs just after sunrise. Officials reported that nearly 1,200 people were injured, with over 40 needing to go to the hospital, mostly because of flying glass from the loud sound created as the meteorite entered the atmosphere. Thankfully, there were no deaths.

The meteorite came into the atmosphere at a speed of at least 33,000 mph and broke up into smaller pieces about 18 to 32 miles above the ground, according to the Russian Academy of Sciences. The event caused panic in Chelyabinsk, a city of one million people located south of the Ural mountains, as the mobile phone networks became overloaded with calls. 

Videos taken by people in the area, often filled with frightened reactions, showed the meteorite pieces glowing brighter as they got closer to the ground. The vapor trail was visible for hundreds of miles, even in neighboring Kazakhstan.

Tatyana Bets was working in a hospital clinic when the meteorite struck. “First, we felt the wind, and then the room filled with bright light and we saw a cloud of smoke in the sky,” she said. After a few minutes, the explosions followed.

At least three craters were found afterward, according to the Ministry of the Interior, and the military was watching the sites. One crater was more than six meters wide, and another piece of the meteorite was reported to have crashed through the thick ice of a nearby lake. Local military officials stated that radiation levels at the impact sites were normal.

An octopus named Inky has made a bold escape from the National Aquarium in New Zealand. He broke out of his tank, crawled down a 50-meter long drainpipe, and swam away into the sea. This event is similar to scenes from the movie *Finding Nemo*.

Inky is a common octopus found in New Zealand, and staff believe he escaped after the lid of his tank was accidentally left open. They think that during the night, while the aquarium was empty, Inky climbed to the top of his tank, slid down the side, and traveled a few meters across the floor. 

Rob Yarrell, the national manager of the aquarium in Napier, said, “Octopuses are known to escape. I don’t think Inky was unhappy or lonely. He is curious and wants to explore what’s outside.” One idea is that Inky crawled to a drainpipe that led straight to the sea. 

When staff arrived the next morning, they were surprised to find that Inky’s tank was empty. Yarrell said, “We were all sad, but this is Inky, and he has always been full of surprises.” Reiss Jenkinson, who takes care of the exhibits, is sure that Inky was not taken by anyone. “I know a lot about how octopuses behave,” he explained. “I have seen them escape from boats. The security here is very tight, so no one could take Inky.”

Octopuses don’t have bones, which allows them to squeeze through really small spaces. They are also very smart and can use tools. At another marine center in Wellington, an octopus was known to sneak into a tank to steal crabs and then come back to its own tank. Another octopus, named Ozymandias, was thought to have set a world record for opening a jar before being released back into the ocean.

Insects are crunchy, high in protein, and can often be found under rocks. While many people worldwide have not traditionally eaten insects, this is starting to change. In Spring 2013, there will be efforts to make insects more popular as food. This includes a special event in London called Pestival 2013, supported by the Wellcome Trust, where people can learn about eating insects. It will feature a temporary restaurant run by the Nordic Food Lab, who had success serving ants at a famous hotel in London.

In March, a BBC documentary will show food writer Stefan Gates trying deep-fried locusts and barbecued tarantulas. There’s a serious point behind this: experts believe eating insects could be good for the environment. Since 2011, the UN’s Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has been promoting the farming and eating of insects, especially in Southeast Asia and Africa, where around two billion people already eat them regularly.

In 2012, the FAO published a list of nearly 2,000 kinds of edible insects and is planning a big international conference on this topic in 2013. As the planet’s population is expected to grow to nine billion in the next 30 years, there will be even more pressure on our land and resources, including water and forests. Currently, one billion people do not get enough food.

The demand for meat is rising, which has two major issues: it requires a lot of land, leading to deforestation, and meat production produces a lot of greenhouse gases. For example, cows contribute significantly to methane emissions, which are harmful to the environment. Livestock farming is responsible for a large portion of the planet’s greenhouse gas emissions, including 35% of methane and 9% of carbon dioxide.

The typical workday of eight hours is finished. Most of the important tasks for the day are done, and anything left can wait until tomorrow. Many workers might think about going home, but in Japan, many employees feel that leaving on time could be seen as unloyal to their company. For many years, businesses have pushed their workers to be as productive as possible. However, the Japanese government is now thinking about a new law that would require employees to take at least five days of paid vacation each year.

Currently, Japanese workers have an average of 18.5 days of paid vacation per year, which is only slightly less than the global average. They also get ten mandatory national holidays. But most workers do not use all their vacation days, often only taking about nine out of their available days. While workers in Britain often expect at least a two-week summer holiday, many Japanese people see a four-day break as a luxury.

This new proposal will be discussed in the current session of parliament. It comes as some companies begin to encourage naps at work to help improve performance. By the end of the decade, the government hopes that this new law will inspire Japanese workers to take their vacations, just like workers in Britain, who take an average of 20 days off, and those in France, who take around 25 days.

While Japan's strong work culture has helped its economy grow, it has often come at the cost of personal lives. The country's low birth rate and expected population decline are partly due to the lack of time people have to start families. More workers are becoming sick from stress or facing serious issues like "karoshi," which means death from overwork. Despite studies showing that longer hours don’t always lead to better productivity, many workers in Japan still feel the pressure from the intense work culture that started during the economic boom in the 1980s.

In John Lewis's Christmas advertisement for 2015, they tell a touching story about a lonely old man living in a crater on the moon. The department store is using this ad to raise money for the charity Age UK, which helps older people. This way, they want to encourage their staff and customers to help lonely elderly people during the holiday season.

The whole campaign costs £7 million and includes not just a TV ad, but also a smartphone game and special merchandise like glow-in-the-dark pajamas. Eleven stores will be decorated to look like the moon. 

The ad features a young girl named Lily. She sees the old man, who lives in a shack on the moon, through her telescope. Lily tries to communicate with him by sending letters and even shooting a note with a bow and arrow. Finally, she sends him a telescope attached to balloons, which allows them to connect. The message of the ad is: “Show someone they’re loved this Christmas,” matching Age UK’s slogan: “No one should have no one at Christmas.” 

Part of the money from sales of products—like a mug, gift tag, and card—will go to the charity. Rachel Swift, the marketing head at John Lewis, said the charity is important to people during Christmas and that it encourages us to think of someone alone nearby.

The ad uses a song called "Half the World Away," performed by the little-known Norwegian singer Aurora. The ad itself cost £1 million to produce. The moon scenes were filmed at Warner Bros Studios, famous for making the Harry Potter films. The set was created by someone who also worked on the latest Star Wars movie, The Force Awakens.

As in previous years, John Lewis built excitement for the ad before its release using a teaser campaign on TV and social media with the hashtag #OnTheMoon. Interestingly, there will be a full moon on Christmas Day in 2015, but this is just a coincidence, according to Swift.

A nasal spray containing the "love hormone" oxytocin may help children with autism manage social situations better, according to researchers in the US. They found that a single dose of oxytocin improved how the brains of children with autism responded to facial expressions. This change could make social interactions feel more natural and rewarding for them. 

The scientists believe that using oxytocin could enhance the effectiveness of already existing behavioral therapies that help people with autism interact socially. "Over time, we would expect to see better social responses, more interest in talking to others, more eye contact, and improved conversation skills," said Kevin Pelphrey, director of the Child Neuroscience Lab at Yale University.

Autism is a developmental disorder affecting more than 1 in 100 people. It varies in how it affects individuals, but it typically involves difficulties in social interaction and communication. Currently, there is no effective treatment for the social challenges associated with autism. Researchers at Yale have focused on oxytocin because it plays an important role in creating bonds and trust.

However, results from previous studies have been mixed; for instance, one recent study found no major benefits for young people receiving oxytocin over several days. Pelphrey argues that oxytocin might help the brain learn from social experiences and may work best when paired with therapies that encourage social engagement.

In their research, scientists used functional MRI scans to observe the brains of 17 youths aged 8 to 16 with autism. The children looked at pictures of either cars or human faces showing different emotions. The scans were taken 45 minutes after they inhaled either a placebo or oxytocin spray. The results showed that after taking oxytocin, the parts of their brains related to pleasure became more active when looking at faces and less active when looking at cars.

This study has been published in the journal *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences* in the US.

Benjamin Carle is 96.9% made in France, even down to his underwear and socks. However, he couldn't be 100% French because he had six Ikea forks, a Chinese guitar, and wall paint that wasn't from a known source. In 2013, at age 26, Carle started an experiment for a TV documentary to see if he could live for ten months using only products made in France. This idea came from a call by a French minister, Arnaud Montebourg, who encouraged people to buy French goods to help the country’s factories.

During the experiment, Carle had to give up his smartphone, television, refrigerator (all made in China), his glasses (from Italy), his underpants (from Morocco), his morning coffee (from Guatemala), and his favorite David Bowie music (from Britain). Luckily, his girlfriend, Anaïs, and their cat, Loon, both French, supported him through this challenge. Carle said, “Politicians make lots of promises, but I wanted to see if it was truly possible to follow their advice and hold them accountable.”

He set three main rules: eat only food made in France, avoid foreign products, and live on €1,800 a month (which is more than the minimum wage of €1,430 in Paris). At the beginning of the experiment, he discovered that only 4.5% of the items in his home were made in France. He had to remove many things, like his lightbulbs (from China) and green beans (from Kenya). After the movers left, his home was almost empty.

Without a refrigerator (none are made in France) or nail clippers, Carle had to keep his food cool on the window ledge and cut his toenails with a penknife. He replaced his foreign-made clothes with more expensive French-made items: underwear for €26, socks for €9, a polo shirt for €75, and espadrille sandals for €26. However, he could not find any French-made jeans.

During the experiment, Carle searched grocery stores for 100% French products, learned to cook seasonal fruits and vegetables from France, was happy to find the last toothbrush made in France from a small company, and washed his clothes by hand until he found a French-made washing machine (which was too big to fit in his kitchen).

The Moroccan city of Ouarzazate is known for hosting big film productions. It's located at the edge of the Sahara Desert and is part of Morocco’s film industry, often called “Ouallywood.” Now, it is also home to four large solar power plants. Together with other sources like hydro and wind energy, these plants are expected to provide almost half of Morocco’s electricity from renewable sources by 2020, with hopes to also sell some of this energy to Europe.

This solar project is part of Morocco’s plan to use its desert areas to become a leader in solar energy. When everything is finished, it will be the largest concentrated solar power plant in the world. The first part, called Noor 1, started operating in November 2015. It uses special mirror technology. While this technology is more expensive and less common than the solar panels found on many rooftops, it has the advantage of producing energy even after sunset.

As engineers complete Noor 1, its 500,000 crescent-shaped mirrors shine brightly in the desert. The mirrors move to follow the sun as it moves across the sky, adjusting their position every few minutes. Once all four solar plants are finished, they will cover an area as large as Morocco’s capital city, Rabat, and generate 580 megawatts (MW) of electricity, enough to power one million homes.

Experts have known for years that deserts receive enough energy each day to meet the world's electricity needs for an entire year. However, the challenge has been capturing that energy and transporting it to cities where it is needed. By 2020, it is expected that solar energy will provide one-third of Morocco’s renewable energy supply, with wind and hydro energy making up the other two-thirds.

Morocco’s environment minister, Hakima el-Haite, expressed pride in this project, stating, “I think it is the most important solar plant in the world.”

A new census shows that there are now more tigers in Nepal than at any time since the 1970s. This is good news for conservationists who have been worried about the decline of these big cats in South Asia for the past 100 years. The latest count found 198 wild Bengal tigers in Nepal, which is a 63.6% increase in just five years. Maheshwar Dhakal, an ecologist from Nepal's Department of National Parks and Wildlife Conservation, called this very encouraging.

The census was done using pictures from over 500 cameras set up in five protected areas and three wildlife corridors. More than 250 conservationists and wildlife experts worked on this survey, which cost about £250,000.

However, increasing wealth in Asia has raised the prices of tiger skins and parts used in traditional Chinese medicine. International gangs pay poor locals in Nepal to kill tigers. The skin and bones are then sent to middlemen, who smuggle them into India where the major buyers are located. A big problem is that some senior officials are involved with these illegal networks.

Experts believe that the rise in tiger numbers is due to better law enforcement in national parks, stronger efforts to stop poaching, and improved management of tiger habitats in Nepal, where forests cover 29% of the land. But they say Nepal needs to do more to protect the habitat and the animals that tigers prey on, so there is enough space and food for the tigers.

As the number of tigers goes up, conflicts with villagers are also increasing. Park officials reported that seven people were killed by tigers near national parks in 2012, compared to four in 2011. Villagers are asking for better protection.

Robert Myslajek stops in his tracks. On a muddy mountain path, he sees something important: "Droppings!" he exclaims. Finding wolf droppings is exciting. Sightings of wolves are very rare, so even seeing their feces is a good sign for someone who tracks animals. More good news is that there are now about 1,500 wolves in Poland—double the number from 15 years ago. Wolves, along with brown bears, lynxes, and wolverines, are among the last big predators in Europe. 

Conservationists from various countries, like Britain, Germany, and the Netherlands, come to Poland to learn how the country has helped save these protected animals, which are often wrongly feared, even in fairy tales. Myslajek, who appears on Polish television, shares that wolves are amazing creatures. They can travel up to 30 kilometers while hunting. He studies a strong wolf pack in the Beskidy mountains, consisting of eight or nine wolves. This year, he has spotted five cubs, two young adults, and two older wolves. He tracks them using special cameras in the forest and by following their tracks in the mud and snow. In each pack, only one pair breeds once a year, but all the pack members help care for the young. 

Myslajek, raised by a shepherd, wonders why wolves have a bad name. "Why is there a term like 'lone-wolf gunman'? Why did we have to hear tales like Little Red Riding Hood and the Big Bad Wolf?" He is intrigued by these reserved animals, which stayed wild 33,000 years ago while other wolves became domestic dogs. Wolves are not pets. Myslajek believes that only scientific reasons—like needing to restore forests and manage wild animal populations—will help protect Europe’s wild predators, especially the often disliked wolf. He explains, “Wild predators help balance nature. They control herbivore numbers, allowing trees to grow tall enough for birds to nest.” 

Hunting wolves was banned in the western Carpathian mountains in 1995, and nationwide in Poland in 1998. Now there are wolf packs in almost all major forests in the country. These predators live alongside humans instead of being kept in enclosures like they are in African safari parks.

Philip Pullman, a well-known author, argues that illegal downloading is wrong and similar to stealing. He believes that taking music or books without paying is just like reaching into someone’s pocket and taking their wallet. In an article for Index on Censorship, Pullman, who leads the Society of Authors, defends copyright laws and criticizes people who think it’s okay to download creative works for free.

Pullman points out that many artists and musicians spend years working hard and often struggle to make a living. Once they finally create something that people enjoy, they risk losing the chance to earn money from it because of piracy. He emphasizes that we should pay for the work of others if we want to enjoy it.

In his article, Pullman also talks about a conversation he has with Cathy Casserly, the head of Creative Commons. Casserly believes that copyright laws are outdated and were made in a time when things were different. She argues that copyright can prevent people from sharing and improving each other’s work, which could not have been foreseen when those laws were created. She encourages artists to think of new ways to share and make money from their creations, emphasizing the idea shared by another writer, Cory Doctorow, who said that his main problem is not piracy, but being overlooked and not recognized.

People from all over Rwanda, and even from some nearby areas in Burundi, travel to the southern town of Butare to visit a small shop called Inzozi Nziza, which means "Sweet Dreams." They come to try ice cream, something many have never tasted before. This shop is the first ice cream parlor in Rwanda, and customers can choose from flavors like sweet cream, passion fruit, strawberry, and pineapple. They can also add toppings like fresh fruit, honey, chocolate chips, and granola. They sell black tea and coffee too.

The shop's manager, Louise Ingabire, believes that ice cream is important for the community. She says, “Some Rwandans like ice cream, but it’s still new to many people. We need to let others know how enjoyable it is.” Inzozi Nziza lives up to its motto—helping people fulfill their dreams. Louise shares, “I didn’t have a job before; I was just at home. Now, I have a vision for my future. I earn money and can help my family.”

Butare has about 89,600 residents and is 135 km south of the capital city, Kigali. It is also home to the National University of Rwanda. Inzozi Nziza has become a popular place for students to relax and enjoy something special. Kalisa Migendo, a 24-year-old agriculture student, says, "It's a place that brings people together. If you want to meet a friend or go on a date, you come to Inzozi Nziza for ice cream."

Most of the ice cream ingredients are sourced locally, and the milk comes from a nearby place in Nyanza. Some ingredients, like vanilla beans and cocoa, are imported.

They call him the Robin Hood of the banks. Enric Duran took out many loans, almost half a million euros, without planning to pay them back. Instead, he gave the money to projects that support alternatives to capitalism. After hiding for 14 months, Duran is proud of what he did, even though he could go to jail for it. “I’m proud of what I did,” he said in a Skype interview from a secret location.

From 2006 to 2008, Duran borrowed money from 39 banks in Spain, getting 68 loans in total. He used the money to support social activists, funding tours against capitalism and media projects. “I saw that these movements were trying to create alternatives, but they didn’t have the money or communication tools they needed,” he explained. “Meanwhile, our economy was growing, creating money out of nothing.” Duran believed that taking these loans was his way of protesting this situation.

He started by filling out loan applications using his real information. His applications were denied, but he wanted to understand how the system worked. Then he began to create a complex plan with different accounts, payments, and transfers. “I was learning all the time,” he said. By the summer of 2007, he figured out how to successfully apply for loans using a fake name for a television company. “Then, I managed to get a lot,” he said—exactly €492,000.

Scientists have used DNA from a European hunter-gatherer's wisdom tooth to learn more about early humans before farming began. This hunter-gatherer lived in Spain about 7,000 years ago and had a unique look: blue eyes, black or brown hair, and dark skin. He likely couldn't digest milk (lactose intolerant) and had more trouble digesting starchy foods compared to farmers who changed diets when they started farming.

With the rise of farming, humans were in closer contact with animals, which may have led to stronger immune systems to fight off diseases that animals passed to humans. However, researchers now think that the impact of farming on the human immune system might have been overestimated. The hunter-gatherer already had genetic traits that helped his immune system deal with various infections, and some of these traits are still found in modern Europeans today.

Lead researcher Carles Lalueza-Fox from the Institute of Evolutionary Biology in Barcelona mentioned that many of their earlier assumptions were incorrect. The study began after cavers discovered two skeletons in a complex cave in the Cantabrian Mountains of northwest Spain in 2006. These skeletons belonged to two men in their early 30s and were well-preserved due to the cool cave environment. Carbon dating showed that the remains were around 7,000 years old, before farming arrived in Europe from the Middle East. This dating matched ancient tools and items found at the site, including reindeer teeth that were likely used as decorations on clothing.

Agios Efstratios is a very remote island in Greece. It is so isolated that banks and the government have basically forgotten about it, and there’s no mobile internet service. There are no ATMs or places that take credit cards on the island. Before Greece faced financial troubles, the residents managed well. They did their banking at the post office, and every summer, people would come to enjoy the beautiful beaches, clear waters, and fresh seafood. 

However, because the island relies only on cash, the closure of banks across Greece has caused big problems. Residents now have to travel nine hours round trip to the nearest big island just to get cash. Many Greek visitors can’t gather enough cash to visit. “Our tourist numbers are down by 80% this year,” said Mayor Maria Kakali, who runs her office in the village where she grew up, which is home to around 200 people. Even people who were born on the island but now live in Athens are not coming back.

Mayor Kakali has spoken to the government and a major Greek bank, and they promised to install an ATM soon. But she worries it may be too late for the busy summer season on an island that mostly depends on tourism, along with fishing and farming. “We have almost no bookings for August. Usually, we get calls from people asking for rooms, but now we can't help them,” she said. 

The coming winter may be made a bit easier by having 50 workers come to the village to help expand the harbor. However, there is an even bigger worry because the government plans to end a tax break that has been helping the island for many years.

Every month in the UK, about 50,000 mystery shopping trips happen, according to the Mystery Shopping Providers Association. As more people shop online, the need for mystery shoppers is increasing. Mystery shoppers act like regular customers in stores, but their real job is to gather information about the service quality.

Simon Boydell from Marketforce says, “Retailers know that customers who visit physical stores want a unique service and experience that online shopping cannot provide.” He added that their clients want to see how well stores are fulfilling that need.

Jill Spencer from ABa, a mystery shopping company, explains, “We give different store locations to each shopper and change them every three months so they don’t return to the same store too soon.” Typically, mystery shoppers spend up to eight hours in one day visiting five to ten stores, plus an hour or two writing reports on their experiences. For this work, they can earn up to £155 per day and get reimbursed for gasoline and hotel stays. Some shoppers drive up to 20,000 miles each year. 

Video mystery shoppers, who record their visits with hidden cameras, can make even more money—around £300 a day. Usually, mystery shoppers are also given money to spend in the stores. Laura, a 50-year-old mystery shopper from Devon, shares, “I usually get between £5 and £20 to spend at each store to see how well they serve me.” The purchase often needs to relate to a specific service or product the store wants her to evaluate. “I am given a scenario, like buying something from a particular department or a new product range, but I can often choose what I want to buy—and I get to keep it.”

Vienna is considered the best city in the world to live in, while Baghdad is the worst, according to a study about quality of life. In this ranking, cities where German is spoken are at the top, with Vienna, Zurich, Munich, Dusseldorf, and Frankfurt all in the top seven. Paris has dropped to 37th place, and London is close behind at 39th. This decline is mainly due to concerns over safety and potential terrorist attacks.

The study looks at factors like social and economic conditions, healthcare, education, housing, and the environment. Companies use this information to decide where to set up offices and how much to pay their employees.

Helena Hartlauer, a 32-year-old from Vienna, wasn't surprised about her city's top ranking. Vienna's government has a strong history of investing in good social housing, making it affordable compared to other major cities. She lives in a large, nice apartment close to the city center, paying just €800 (about £625) a month. In contrast, a similar apartment in London would cost at least £2,000, while New York is ranked even lower at 44th place.

Cities in the US generally score lower in this study due to concerns about safety and crime. San Francisco is the highest-ranking US city at 28th place, while Boston is 34th. Canadian cities, like Vancouver, perform much better than US cities in this ranking.

“I didn’t realize how safe Vienna is until I traveled to other places,” said Hartlauer. “We also have great public transportation, like the underground train that runs 24 hours on weekends, and it only costs €1 per trip.”

Clay Cockrell works as a therapist in his office near Columbus Circle, across from Trump International Hotel and Tower. He holds special therapy sessions in Central Park, where he walks and talks with his clients. Cockrell used to work on Wall Street but decided to become a therapist, focusing on very wealthy people. He explains, “I naturally moved toward this area of therapy.” 

Many wealthy individuals, especially those in the top 1%, often feel that their problems are not real problems, but they are. Cockrell points out several issues these people face: “They often feel guilty about being rich, think they need to hide their wealth, and experience loneliness because of their position.” This reminds us of what writer F. Scott Fitzgerald said: the very rich are different from everyone else.

Experts believe these issues have worsened since the financial crisis and the discussions about income inequality brought up by movements like Occupy Wall Street and the Fight for $15 campaign. Jamie Traeger-Muney, a psychologist who specializes in wealth, notes, “While the Occupy Wall Street movement raised important issues about income inequality, it also portrayed the wealthy in a negative light.” She believes the media has contributed to making rich people feel like they should hide or be ashamed of their wealth. “Sometimes, I am surprised by how people talk about wealthy individuals. It seems acceptable to speak about them in ways we wouldn’t use for any other group.”

Swedish prisons are known for being progressive and different from prisons in many other countries. In November 2013, Nils Oberg, the head of Sweden’s prison and probation service, announced that four prisons would close because fewer people were in jail. Between 2011 and 2012, the number of prisoners in Sweden dropped by 6%, even though crime rates did not fall. Oberg is unsure why this happened but believes it may be linked to how prisons focus on rehabilitation and preventing reoffending.

Kenneth Gustafsson, who is the governor of Kumla Prison (Sweden's most secure prison), agrees that the system is not "soft." He noted that when he started working in 1978, prisons were designed to be humane, and inmates were treated well. However, after some high-profile escapes in 2004, security measures had to be tightened.

In Sweden, prison sentences tend to be shorter, usually not longer than ten years. The country was the first in Europe to use electronic tagging for criminals and tries to avoid short prison terms by using community-based punishments, which help reduce reoffending more effectively. 

The rate of reoffending in Sweden is about 30 to 40% over three years, which is lower than in the UK, where the numbers are much higher. One reason for Sweden's lower incarceration rate (less than 70 per 100,000 people) is that the age of criminal responsibility is 15. In the UK, young people ages 10 to 17 have high reoffending rates, and many eventually go to adult prisons. In Sweden, no one under 21 can receive a life sentence, and the system works hard to keep young offenders out of prison.

Many people know they don’t get enough sleep, but what if there was a simple solution? Dr. Paul Kelley, a researcher from Oxford University, spoke at the British Science Festival about changing school start times to match students' natural sleep patterns. He believes this could help improve their learning, test scores, and overall health. Lack of sleep is connected to serious issues like diabetes, depression, and obesity.

Dr. Kelley shared that around age 10, children naturally wake up around 6:30 AM. By age 16, this time shifts to 8:00 AM, and by 18, it becomes 9:00 AM. This means that starting school early in the morning works for young children but not for older teenagers. He suggests that schools might consider starting classes at 11:00 AM or later for teens.

He also points out that waking older teens at 7:00 AM is like making a 50-year-old teacher wake up at 4:30 AM. It’s not just about telling teenagers to go to bed earlier. Dr. Kelley explains that our bodies have a natural rhythm controlled by light. Special cells in our eyes send signals to a part of the brain that helps manage our sleep-wake cycle. It’s similar to saying you can’t control your heartbeat; it’s something your body does naturally.
